title: NOC 72401 – Heavy-duty equipment mechanics | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Heavy-duty Equipment Mechanics (NOC 72401) are the vital technicians ensuring the reliability of Canada’s massive mobile infrastructure. From the 400-tonne haul trucks in the Alberta oil sands to the sophisticated harvesters in the Prairies and excavators in Ontario's transit projects, these professionals are in higher demand than ever. Operating at the TEER 2 level, the role has moved beyond pure "wrenching" into advanced digital diagnostics. In 2026, a top-tier mechanic must be as comfortable with a diagnostic laptop and high-pressure hydraulic sensors as they are with heavy rigging and welding.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Category2 (Requires college, apprenticeship, or significant experience)Major Group72 – Technical trades and transportation officersSub-Major Group724 – Machine repair and maintenance trades

duties: Advanced Diagnostics: Diagnosing faults or malfunctions using computerized testing equipment to determine the extent of repair required.
Precision Repair: Adjusting equipment and repairing or replacing defective parts, components, or systems using specialized power tools and heavy rigging.
System Testing: Testing repaired equipment for proper performance to ensure it meets strict 2026 manufacturer and environmental specifications.
Maintenance: Cleaning, lubricating, and performing routine preventive maintenance on heavy mobile units.
Specialized Service: Servicing attachments and working tools such as blades, ploughs, winches, side booms, and harvesting equipment.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school and a vocational program is usually required.
Apprenticeship: Completion of a three- to five-year apprenticeship program OR a combination of over four years of work experience and industry courses is required for certification.
Certification: Trade certification is compulsory in Alberta and Quebec (construction only). It is voluntary but highly recommended in all other provinces.
Red Seal: The Interprovincial Standards Red Seal is the "Gold Standard," allowing you to work in any province and access high-paying union and mining roles.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is Moderate to Strong, specifically driven by the "Infrastructure 2030" plan and critical mineral mining:

Ontario: Very Good prospects. On February 18, 2026, Ontario issued targeted OINP invitations for NOC 72401 under the Employer Job Offer: Foreign Worker stream. The minimum score required was 50, highlighting an urgent need for mechanics to support highway, transit, and power projects across the province.

Alberta: Highest Demand & Compulsory Trade. Alberta is one of the few provinces where trade certification is compulsory. It offers the highest wages, with a median of $41.00/hr and top earners in the mining sector exceeding $102,000 annually.

British Columbia: High Priority. This NOC is currently on the BC PNP Priority Construction Occupations List. Candidates with a valid trade certificate from SkilledTradesBC and a job offer benefit from targeted draws and faster PR processing.
National Trend: Over 95% of workers in this NOC are men, though 2026 has seen a significant federal push (with grants up to $3,000) to recruit women and underrepresented groups into heavy mechanics.
Why Work in Canada as a Heavy-duty Mechanic?
Technological Mastery: Work with 2026-grade innovations, including autonomous mining fleets, Tier 5 emission-control engines, and AI-driven predictive maintenance.
Recession-Resistant Skills: Your expertise is required across multiple essential sectors: mining (32% in some regions), construction (16%), and agriculture.
Elite Compensation: As a TEER 2 trade, your median hourly wage in Canada is $37.12/hr, with experienced journeypersons in remote northern regions earning upwards of $60.00/hr.
Priority for PR: In early 2026, this NOC remains a core target for the Express Entry "Trade Occupations" category, which now requires 12 months of experience.
pathways: OINP Employer Job Offer: In February 2026, OINP issued invitations to candidates in NOC 72401 with a score of 50+ under the Foreign Worker stream. This is currently the most viable route for those with an Ontario job offer.
Express Entry (Trade Occupations Category): Updated for 2026. Eligible candidates now require 12 months of full-time work experience (increased from 6 months in 2025) in a trade.
BC PNP Construction Stream: As a Priority Occupation, heavy mechanics benefit from targeted draws and faster processing if they have a job offer and are registered with SkilledTradesBC.
2026 TR to PR Pathway: A confirmed initiative targeting up to 33,000 temporary residents. Heavy-duty mechanics are classified as "High-Priority Essential Infrastructure Workers."
